nodes.label: AI for repetitive simple tasks
nodes.label_negative: Manual coding for repetitive simple tasks
nodes.grounding_span: When something has simple logic but just needs repetition, I would use AI instead of spending a lot of time in writing it myself.

nodes.label: AI as collaborative partner
nodes.label_negative: AI for complex tasks without iterative collaboration
nodes.grounding_span: For harder tasks that involve a bigger codebase, complex logic, or more intense reasoning, my initial prompt tends to be longer and more complicated. I would then go back and forth with the AI.

nodes.label: AI reliability for practical tasks
nodes.grounding_span: As AI is not fully accurate and is still prone to error due to the aforementioned reasons I gave

nodes.label: AI for security-sensitive high-impact tasks
nodes.label_negative: Manual coding for security-sensitive high-impact tasks
nodes.grounding_span: I sometimes still prefer to write code for short but important tasks. Important in the sense that the task is security-sensitive or has high impact on the organisation.

nodes.label: AI for security-sensitive high-impact tasks
nodes.grounding_span: our organisation has a huge security concern around AI, in particular, agent to agent protocols, MCP servers, and in general, AI code generation.

nodes.label: AI impact on skill maintenance
nodes.label_negative: AI as skill maintenance neutral or beneficial
nodes.grounding_span: I actively think about how my skills could deteriorate due to overuse of AI.

nodes.label: AI as skill requiring prompt engineering
nodes.grounding_span: it also requires people to have a new skill, prompt engineering. You also have to be able to read code to identify if the AI has done what you want.

nodes.label: Productivity and efficiency
nodes.grounding_span: it saved me a lot of time

nodes.label: Security and organizational integrity
nodes.grounding_span: AI security must constantly be in the back of mind

nodes.label: Personal competence and skill development
nodes.grounding_span: I actively think about how my skills could deteriorate due to overuse of AI.

nodes.label: Satisfaction with AI as a helpful tool
nodes.grounding_span: I felt really satisfied, as it saved me a lot of time.

nodes.label: Frustration with AI for niche problems
nodes.grounding_span: if the task is too niche or too complex, the AI may not be able to fulfill the task. In those cases, I need to manually write the code

nodes.label: Cautious trust in AI
nodes.grounding_span: one prompt tends to do the trick. I would then just check that the AI has done it correctly.

nodes.label: Preference for manual coding for security-sensitive tasks
nodes.grounding_span: I sometimes still prefer to write code for short but important tasks. I write it myself because I would be more confident in the final result.

nodes.label: Caution toward AI in high-risk tasks
nodes.grounding_span: AI security must constantly be in the back of mind

nodes.label: Experience-driven pattern recognition of AI limitations
nodes.grounding_span: It is due to my experience with using LLMs and the errors that they struggle with in the past.

nodes.label: Iterative step-by-step problem decomposition
nodes.grounding_span: I work with the AI to help it unfold those layers of reasoning by providing it verbose explanations or additional context.
